nuggets.headline: Radiohead's partnership with Nigel Godrich solidified while their producer was at a party
nuggets.text: During sessions for *The Bends*, producer John Leckie left RAK Studios for a social engagement. The band stayed behind with engineer Nigel Godrich to work on B-sides, but one of the tracks they recorded, "Black Star," was so strong it made the album, cementing the creative trust that led to Godrich producing every Radiohead record since.

source.title: Nigel Godrich
source.quoteSnippet: When Leckie left the studio to attend a social engagement, Radiohead and Godrich stayed to record B-sides. One song, "Black Star", was instead included on The Bends.

nuggets.imageCaption: Producer Nigel Godrich, Radiohead's long-term creative partner and de facto sixth member.

nuggets.headline: The drums in "Weird Fishes/Arpeggi" deliberately avoid signaling song structure
nuggets.text: While most rock songs use drum fills and pattern changes to mark transitions between verse and chorus, Phil Selway’s groove on this track remains almost entirely constant. The band builds momentum texturally by layering the three intricate guitar arpeggios, rather than rhythmically — a choice that contributes to the song's hypnotic, flowing feel.

source.title: A Harmonic Analysis of Radiohead’s “Weird Fishes/Arpeggi” – Flypaper
source.quoteSnippet: The track starts with a solid drum groove, which will stay the same throughout most of the song. This choice is quite interesting since it implies that the drums will not signal the subdivision of the arrangement in sections, as is usually how drum arrangements function in pop music.

nuggets.imageCaption: A transcription of Ed O'Brien's opening guitar arpeggio in the track.

nuggets.headline: Radiohead's *In Rainbows* could have sounded more like Muse's *Black Holes and Revelations*
nuggets.text: Before reuniting with Nigel Godrich, Radiohead held sessions for *In Rainbows* with producer Mark "Spike" Stent. Stent is known for his work with Muse, but the sessions were a "dead end" and ultimately scrapped, leading the band back to Godrich to start the album from scratch. For a glimpse of that alternate-reality album, listen to Muse's "Starlight."

source.title: The Making of Radiohead's 'In Rainbows'
source.quoteSnippet: The band finally got back to work in early 2005, recording on their own and later with producer Mark “Spike” Stent... But the sessions – their first in a decade with a producer other than Godrich... were a dead end.

artistSummary: Formed in Abingdon, Oxfordshire, Radiohead have spent their career defying expectations, evolving from 90s alternative rock standard-bearers into experimental electronic pioneers. The core five-piece, alongside longtime producer Nigel Godrich, consistently challenges the boundaries of genre and the conventions of the music industry itself.
